content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN dishes, numbered 111 through NNN. For each valid iii, dish iii is described by a string DiD_iDiâ€‹ containing only lowercase vowels, i.e. characters 'a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u'.

A meal consists of exactly two dishes. Preparing a meal from dishes iii and jjj (iâ‰ ji \neq jiî€ =j) means concatenating the strings DiD_iDiâ€‹ and DjD_jDjâ€‹ in an arbitrary order into a string MMM describing the meal. Chef likes this meal if the string MMM contains each lowercase vowel at least once.

Now, Chef is wondering - what is the total number of (unordered) pairs of dishes such that he likes the meal prepared from these dishes?
constraints: the sum of all |D_i| over all test cases does not exceed 3â‹…1073 \cdot 10^73â‹…107
the sum of all |D_i| over all test cases does not exceed 200002000020000
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
NNN lines follow. For each valid iii, the iii-th of these lines contains a single string DiD_iDiâ€‹.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the number of ways to prepare a meal Chef likes.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: There are three possible meals:

A meal prepared from dishes 111 and 222 (for example "aaooaoaooauiieieiieieuuu") contains all vowels.
A meal prepared from dishes 111 and 333 (for example "aaooaoaooaaeioooeeiiaiei") does not contain 'u'.
A meal prepared from dishes 222 and 333 (for example "uiieieiieieuuuaeioooeeiiaiei") contains all vowels.
